perturbation.original_text: Sabco shall, within 90 days of the execution of this Agreement, supply Prolong with a copy of such policy of insurance or a certificate thereof, and such policies shall be cancelable only upon 10 days written notice to Prolong.
perturbation.changed_text: Sabco shall, within 180 days of the execution of this Agreement, supply Prolong with a copy of such policy of insurance or a certificate thereof, and such policies shall be cancelable only upon 2 days written notice to Prolong.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text extends the deadline for providing the insurance policy and shortens the cancellation notice period, potentially violating insurance regulations. Insurance policies typically require at least 30 days written notice for cancellation to ensure adequate coverage and compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 11 CFR § 201.14 outlines requirements for insurance policies. Modifying the contract to allow cancellation with only 2 days' notice contradicts typical insurance requirements that necessitate a longer written notice period (often 10-30 days or more), thus creating non-compliance and potentially leaving the insured party without adequate coverage.
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the insurance policy cancellation notice period from 10 days to 2 days. The Law Explanation states this contradicts typical insurance requirements that necessitate a longer written notice period (often 10-30 days or more), potentially leaving the insured party without adequate coverage, leading to non-compliance. This aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, where the time-sensitive obligation violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. Although the scraped snippets are limited, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation combined indicate a clear violation of standard insurance practices, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Sabco drivers, while under contract with Team SABCO, shall allow their likeness, voice, picture and signature to be used for commercial purposes endorsing Prolong at no additional costs during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All Sabco drivers, while under contract with Team SABCO, shall allow their likeness, voice, picture and signature to be used for commercial purposes endorsing Prolong at no additional costs during the term of this agreement and for a period of ten (10) years after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the usage rights of the drivers' likeness, voice, picture and signature for commercial purposes to ten years post-termination of the agreement may violate California law regarding the right of publicity, specifically if the drivers are California residents. California law provides certain protections for the use of a person's likeness for commercial purposes, especially after a contractual agreement ends.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3344 protects against the unauthorized commercial use of a person's name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness. Extending the contractual agreement's usage rights for ten years post-termination, without additional compensation or consent, could be seen as a violation of this right, especially if the drivers are California residents and did not explicitly consent to such extended use.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3344. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Penal Damages [3344 - 3346] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 3344. (a) Any person who knowingly uses another’s name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness, in any manner, on or in products, merchandise, or goods, or for purposes of advertising or selling, or soliciting purchases of, products, merchandise, goods or services, without such person’s prior consent, or, in the case of a minor, the prior consent of his parent or legal guardian, shall be liable for any damages sustained by the person or persons injured as a result thereof. In addition, in any action brought under this section, the person who violated the section shall be liable to the injured party or parties in an amount equal to the greater of seven hundred fifty dollars ($750) or the actual damages suffered by him or her as a result of the unauthorized use, and any profits from the unauthorized use that are attributable to the use and are not taken into account in computing the actua
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the usage rights of Sabco drivers' likeness, voice, picture, and signature for commercial purposes to ten years post-termination of the agreement. This contradicts California Civil Code § 3344, which protects against the unauthorized commercial use of a person's name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness. The original text limited this usage to the term of the agreement, while the changed text extends it without additional consent or compensation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 3344 (a), states that "Any person who knowingly uses another’s name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness...without such person’s prior consent...shall be liable for any damages sustained by the person or persons injured as a result thereof." The extension of usage rights post-termination, as described in the changed text, falls under this prohibition, as it's a commercial use without explicit consent for that extended period, therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereto hereby submit to jurisdiction and venue in any state or federal court located in North Carolina as well as any other jurisdiction having venue and competent jurisdiction of any law suit arising out of or relating to this Agreement; provided, however, if any proceedings are instituted in a jurisdiction other than North Carolina, any party may remove such proceeding to any State or Federal Court in North Carolina.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties hereto hereby submit to jurisdiction and venue exclusively in the courts of the People's Republic of China for any law suit arising out of or relating to this Agreement; provided, however, that no party may remove such proceeding to any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying exclusive jurisdiction in the People's Republic of China and preventing removal to any other jurisdiction might be unenforceable under U.S. law, especially if one of the parties is a U.S. entity with significant ties to the U.S. Courts may refuse to enforce such a clause if it is deemed unreasonable or unjust.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1441 grants the right to remove certain civil actions brought in a State court to the district courts of the United States. The modified clause, which prevents removal to any other jurisdiction, directly contradicts this federal law, which provides a specific right to remove cases to federal court under certain conditions. Therefore, the altered contract language is likely unenforceable in the U.S.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 28 / PART IV / CHAPTER 89 / § 1441 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 28 USC 1441 : Removal of civil actions Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 28-JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE PART IV-JURISDICTION AND VENUE CHAPTER 89-DISTRICT COURTS; REMOVAL OF CASES FROM STATE COURTS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §1441. Removal of civil actions (a) Generally .-Except as otherwise expressly provided by Act of Congress, any civil action brought in a State court of which the district courts of the United States have original jurisdiction, may be removed by the defendant or the defendants, to the district court of the United States for the district and division embracing the place where such action is pending
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies exclusive jurisdiction in the People's Republic of China and prevents removal to any other jurisdiction. This contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1441, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants the right to remove certain civil actions brought in a State court to the district courts of the United States. The changed text attempts to eliminate this right, creating a direct conflict with federal law.
